summary: The Gemara concludes the wood-arrangement debate: Rava objects that if wood arrangement is complete service then it requires a lottery too - contradiction resolved. Rabbi Yochanan's final position is that two log-placement is a complete service (avodah temimah) for death-penalty purposes. The page then presents the Mishna opening Yom Kippur's Temple service: the appointed priest checks if dawn has broken. Priests are taken to the Hall of Immersion for the High Priest's first immersion.

glossary.en: Dawn; the Yom Kippur avodah begins only after dawn is confirmed.
Hall of Parva; the chamber containing the immersion pool used by the High Priest on Yom Kippur.
Appointed official; the Temple superintendent who coordinated service timing.

rashiTranslations.en: Continuation: wonder at R. Zeira's argument about wood-arrangement and burning of incense.
Revision of R. Yochanan's position: the statement applies only to the two-log placement.
Rava's objection: if placing the two logs is complete service, it should require its own lottery.
Rava's actual position: the two-log placement is complete service and thus requires priesthood.
Question: is there any complete service by a non-priest that is valid but punishable?
Further question about complete service and the lottery requirement.
Why R. Yochanan's teaching is framed around slaughtering in the mishna.
Rejection: the mishna mentions slaughter because slaughter is simply the first step listed.
Blank or gap in the Sefaria sequence.
Mishna: Appointed priest tells priests - go out and see if it is dawn yet.
The High Priest is brought to the Hall of Immersion for his first immersion of Yom Kippur.
Among the services for which a non-kohen is liable.
Because it is followed by the limb arrangement.
But the time of the two fire-logs is not taught - implying it is a nighttime service.
And it completes the fire arrangement.
The limb arrangement which is a daytime service is the start of a new service.
Not the completion of the fire arrangement.
End of chapter - the second chapter concludes.
The Hadran.
Chapter Three: The Superintendent said - this refers to the Deputy KG.
Go out and see - onto the wall or the roof.
The time for slaughter - slaughter of the tamid.
Meaning: has dawn broken, since slaughter is invalid at night.
As written (Lev. 19) 'on the day of your sacrifice.'
The one who sees - the one who went up to the roof.
'Berkai' - the word means brightness.
As in 'to give it a brightness' (Ezek. 21) and 'the lightning lit up' (Ps. 77).
'Until it reaches Hebron' - until the light is visible as far as Hebron.
And he replied 'yes' - the Gemara explains.
Why did they need to do this - to ascend to the roof and check?
The moonlight of early dawn glowed.
The moon's form appeared close to morning time.
And they thought - they assumed it was dawn.
'They took him to the burning place' - when they realized it was slaughtered at night.
They brought the KG down - explained in the Gemara.
Easing his bodily needs - refers to the large needs.
The term 'to ease' is like 'a tree covering the ground' (Nazir 54a).
Since for the large needs one crouches on one's knees.
And covers one's feet with the body.
As written about Saul (1 Sam. 24) 'and Saul went in to cover his feet.'
Sanctification of hands and feet - from the laver.
He sanctified hands and feet from the laver before the first service.
Sanctification of hands and feet is from the laver.
Sanctification of hands and feet is from the laver.
Gemara begins.

display.whats: The wood-arrangement debate concludes: the two-log placement is a complete service requiring priesthood. Then the Mishna opens: dawn is announced, the High Priest descends to his first immersion of Yom Kippur morning.
display.hint: The wood-arrangement debate ends with Rava's position that two-log placement counts as a complete service. The Yom Kippur narrative then begins in earnest.

learning.summary: Rava objects to the revised version of R. Yochanan's ruling: if two-log placement is a complete service (avodah temimah), it should require its own lottery - but it doesn't. Rava's actual position is clarified: the two-log placement IS complete service and therefore requires priestly status, meaning a non-priest doing it is both punished and invalidates the service. The Gemara then transitions to the Mishna: the appointed Temple official sends priests to check if dawn has broken. Once dawn is confirmed, the High Priest descends to the Hall of Parva for his first of five Yom Kippur immersions.
learning.ahaMoment: The wood-arrangement debate was actually preparing for the Yom Kippur narrative: understanding which services are 'complete' (requiring priests) becomes critical as the High Priest's unique Yom Kippur service begins.
learning.memoryAnchor: Two logs on the altar = complete priestly service. Then: first immersion begins the Yom Kippur avodah.
learning.openQuestion: Why does the Yom Kippur service begin with a dawn check? What would happen if the night-before preparations accidentally continued past dawn without anyone noticing?

argumentFlow.label: Rava objects to revised R. Yochanan
argumentFlow.text: Rava: if two-log placement is complete service and valid when done by a priest, it should also require its own lottery - but it does not. This is a contradiction.

argumentFlow.label: Rava's actual position
argumentFlow.text: Rava clarifies: two-log placement IS complete service - a non-priest is punished and the service is invalidated. The lottery question is separately resolved.

argumentFlow.label: Mishna referenced to explain R. Yochanan's framing
argumentFlow.text: The mishna mentions slaughter as its starting point simply because slaughter is the first temple task listed, not because it is the key contested service.

argumentFlow.label: Mishna: Dawn check begins Yom Kippur
argumentFlow.text: The appointed priest announces: go out and see if dawn has broken. Once confirmed, the Yom Kippur avodah begins.

argumentFlow.label: First immersion: Hall of Parva
argumentFlow.text: The High Priest is taken down to the Hall of Parva for his first immersion before beginning Yom Kippur service.

theological: dawn as threshold for divine service

misconceptions: The two-log placement can be done by a non-priest since it doesn't require a lottery - Rava establishes it IS complete priestly service.
The Yom Kippur service can begin at any time of night - it requires dawn confirmation first.

visualizableElements.label: Yom Kippur service initiation
visualizableElements.description: Dawn check - announcement - priests lead High Priest to Hall of Parva - first immersion

visualizableElements.label: Hall of Parva
visualizableElements.description: The immersion chamber where the High Priest underwent his Yom Kippur immersions

quizSeeds.q: What is Rava's position on whether two-log placement counts as 'complete Temple service'?
quizSeeds.a: Rava holds that placing the two logs on the altar fire IS complete Temple service - a non-priest doing it is both punished and the service is invalidated.

quizSeeds.q: What is the first step in the Yom Kippur morning service according to the Mishna?
quizSeeds.a: The appointed official sends priests out to check if dawn has broken. Only after dawn is confirmed does the Yom Kippur avodah begin.

sugyot.finalRuling: Two-log placement is complete priestly service. The Yom Kippur avodah begins at dawn with the High Priest's first immersion.
